Glossary About
Renaissance & Late Scholasticism

Francisco Suárez

1548 – 1617

The late scholastic synthesis: soul, intellect, and substance on the threshold of modernity.

Francisco Suárez on soul and mind: an in-depth reconstruction

1. Central claim

A comprehensive philosophy of mind can be extracted from Suárez, but only if “mind” is not narrowed to post-Cartesian consciousness or to a merely human psychology. Suárez’s theory is a hylomorphic, powers-based, act-centered, and comparative theory of mindedness. Human mind is the rational soul as the substantial form of a living body, operating through distinct but ordered powers: sense, inner sense, intellect, appetite, and will. Mental acts are not merely passive receptions; they are vital, immanent acts of a living subject. Representation is mediated by intentional species, but species do not replace the living power’s own activity. Consciousness is layered: there is sensory self-presence, inner-sense awareness, intellectual reflection, and the higher self-knowledge proper to separated or divine intellects.

God, angels, and separated souls are not peripheral to this theory. Suárez himself distinguishes the study of the human intellect “as understanding in a body” from intellect considered absolutely, where the subject includes separated substances and especially the first intellect. That means divine and angelic minds are limit cases in Suárez’s general theory of intellect, not accidental theological ornaments. They help define what human mind is by contrast: embodied, phantasm-dependent in this life, yet spiritual, reflexive, and not reducible to an organ.

Suárez’s psychology belongs to his wider philosophical project. He is a central figure of Second Scholasticism, active in metaphysics, natural theology, philosophy of mind, action theory, ethics, politics, and law, and his influence extends into early modern philosophy through figures such as Leibniz, Grotius, Pufendorf, Schopenhauer, and Heidegger. The point is not that Suárez is secretly a modern analytic philosopher of mind, but that his system contains a sophisticated theory of cognition, intentionality, consciousness, embodiment, agency, freedom, and nonhuman intellect.

2. Terminological and methodological cautions

Suárez does not write a “philosophy of mind” in the modern departmental sense. His central terms are anima, mens, intellectus, ratio, voluntas, potentia, species, phantasma, cognitio, appetitus, and actus. “Soul” is broader than “mind”: it names the principle of life in plants, animals, and human beings. “Mind” in the narrower sense is principally the rational or intellectual dimension of the soul: intellect and will. But Suárez’s account of mind cannot be separated from sensitive cognition, inner sense, imagination, memory, appetite, bodily dependence, and the life-principle that unifies them.

The main textual base is Suárez’s commentary and disputations on Aristotle’s De anima, especially the Castellote edition. The third volume, where online access is more fragmentary, identifies itself as a copy of Francisco Suárez, Commentaria una cum quaestionibus in libros Aristotelis De Anima, volume III, edited by Salvador Castellote and published in Madrid in 1991. I also use Suárez’s metaphysical framework, especially where it bears on beings of reason, intentionality, and the ontology of powers and acts. Contemporary interpretation is now multilingual: Spanish-language scholarship on the agent intellect and voluntary self-determination, English and German work on consciousness and self-awareness, and broader European work on Suárez’s psychology all matter here. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy, for example, highlights Dominik Perler’s work on Suárez’s distinction between first-order sensory consciousness and second-order intellectual consciousness, and the Conimbricenses bibliography points to work by Knuuttila, Rode, Renemann, Tropia, and others on psychology, inner experience, divine and human intellection, and the connections between vital acts.

A second caution concerns the disciplinary location of Suárez’s psychology. “Physics” in this context means Aristotelian natural philosophy, not modern mathematical physics. Suárez explicitly rejects a clean division in which the rational soul belongs to metaphysics only as intellective, while natural philosophy treats it only as vegetative and sensitive. The rational soul, precisely as principle of understanding, is the soul of the body and constitutes the human being; therefore it belongs to natural philosophy absolutely, and also in a qualified way to metaphysics. This is crucial: Suárez’s theory of mind is neither a purely theological doctrine nor a purely empirical psychology. It is natural-philosophical, metaphysical, and theological at once.

3. The science of the soul

Suárez treats the science of the soul as a foundational inquiry into living beings and into the operations by which humans know themselves, the world, and God. He insists that the science of the soul studies the acts of intellect, the intellectual light, and the object of intellect; it teaches reflection on acts and intellectual light and is useful for logic and dialectic. He also says that from knowledge of our own soul and intellect we ascend to some knowledge of God and separated substances.

This ascent is not a decorative pious conclusion. It is built into the structure of the inquiry. To understand human mind, Suárez must ask what intellect is in general, what distinguishes embodied intellect from separated intellect, how intellectual acts differ from sensory acts, and how the human soul can be both form of a body and capable of spiritual operations. He explicitly says that every being with intellect naturally benefits from self-knowledge, that an angel constantly knows itself, and that human beings especially know themselves through the science of the soul.

For Suárez, then, psychology has three intertwined aims. First, it explains living beings as organized substances. Second, it explains cognition and appetite as operations of living powers. Third, it uses human self-knowledge as a route toward a more general theory of intellect, including the intellects of angels and God. That third aim is not a category error. It is one reason his philosophy of mind is more expansive than many modern accounts.

4. Soul as substantial form, life-principle, and unity-principle

Suárez inherits the Aristotelian thesis that the soul is the first act of a living body. The soul is the substantial form of a living organism and the primary principle of life. Contemporary summaries of Suárez’s psychology emphasize that he defines the soul as the principle of life and unity, while powers such as intellect and will are defined by their acts: intellect by thinking, will by willing. Spanish scholarship on Suárez’s epistemology likewise begins from the Aristotelian tripartition of vegetative, sensitive, and rational soul, and describes the soul as distinct from the body while functioning as the first principle of perfections and operations in the living being.

This gives Suárez a non-reductive account of mind. The mind is not a bundle of psychological events. Nor is it a detachable Cartesian ego that happens to be associated with a body. Human mind is the rational soul considered through its intellectual and voluntary powers, and that soul is the form that makes the human body one living human being. The human being is therefore not body plus mind as two complete substances. It is one substance whose substantial form is rational, spiritual, and capable of operations that exceed bodily organs.

Suárez is especially concerned to preserve the unity of the human subject. He holds that the indivisible soul is whole in the whole body and whole in each part, while distinguishing several senses of totality: essential, facultative, and quantitative. This is not a claim that every part of the body thinks. Rather, the soul as substantial form informs the whole living body, while particular powers may require particular organs. The intellect is not located in a bodily part in the way vision is located in the eye. Suárez says that intellect is not in a bodily part but in the soul; one may speak of it as “in the head” only improperly, because the organs of the senses and the phantasms of imagination are located there.

That thesis is fundamental for his philosophy of mind. Suárez wants to explain why human thinking depends on bodily sense and imagination in this life, without making thinking itself an operation of the brain or of a sensory organ. He therefore gives an embodied but non-materialist account: the human intellect is the intellect of an embodied soul, but its formal operation is spiritual.

5. Spirituality, embodiment, and the proof from intellectual acts

Suárez argues for the immateriality of the rational soul from the character of intellectual and voluntary acts. The intellect knows material things spiritually: it abstracts from place, time, corruptibility, sensible accidents, and individual material conditions, arriving at universal natures. For Suárez, such cognition reveals a spiritual faculty and a spiritual principle.

He adds several further arguments. The intellect can reflect on itself, its acts, its species, and its intellectual light. It can judge, reason discursively, infer from principles, know truth, compare past and future, and think even of nonentities. The will also testifies to spirituality: it has free acts, can resist sensory attractions, and is not determined simply by created natural causes. Suárez argues that intellect and will rest finally only in the supreme and infinite good, which shows that their formal range exceeds finite bodily objects.

These arguments converge in the claim that the intellective principle is immortal by natural reason. Suárez does not rely on one isolated argument; he says that arguments from the properties and acts of intellect and will jointly form an excellent demonstration. At the same time, he does not let immateriality sever the soul from embodiment. He stresses that if intellect were not truly our faculty, then intellection would not be ours; he also notes that experience shows understanding depends on the senses, since bodily fatigue and impaired sensory functioning impede intellectual activity. For him, that dependence is evidence that the intellective principle is the true form of the body, not a foreign mind merely attached to it.

The result is a delicate position. Human thinking is not reducible to sense or brain; nevertheless, in the present embodied state, intellectual knowledge normally requires sensory cognition and phantasms. Suárez’s human mind is neither a pure angelic intellect nor an animal sensory system. It is an embodied spiritual power.

6. Powers of the soul: real distinction, dependence, hierarchy

Suárez’s philosophy of mind is a theory of powers. The soul is not identical with each of its powers; powers are really distinct from one another and from the soul. They are intrinsic accidental forms, stable capacities rooted in the soul, dependent on it, and ordered hierarchically. The soul produces them efficiently and functions as the primary causal and coordinating principle of their operations.

This is one of Suárez’s most important contributions to a philosophy of mind. The mental architecture is not flat. There are vegetative powers, external senses, internal senses, intellect, appetite, and will. These are not merely names for behaviors; they are real principles of operation. Yet they are not independent substances. They belong to one living subject and are coordinated by the soul.

Suárez sharply distinguishes spiritual and sensory faculties. Spiritual faculties such as intellect and will are sustained immediately in the soul. Sensory faculties are not immediately in the soul in the same way; if they were, sensation would itself be spiritual and the senses would be spiritual powers, which Suárez rejects. His account therefore avoids two extremes: reducing intellect to sensory processing and spiritualizing all sensation.

The relation among powers is also subtle. Suárez rejects a simple picture in which lower powers efficiently cause higher powers to act. He denies that sensory powers directly produce intellectual acts or intelligible species. At most, lower operations can serve as occasions or quasi-exemplars for higher operations. The soul coordinates the powers and secures unity of cognition and action. This is why his psychology is sometimes read as a theory of “sympathy” or ordered harmony among powers rather than a straightforward upward causal mechanism from sense to intellect.

7. The representational structure of cognition: species, acts, and objects

Suárez’s theory of cognition is intentionalist, but not in a passive or merely pictorial sense. A cognitive act is not simply the reception of an image. Suárez describes knowledge as a “conjunction of object with power,” and he insists that intentional species are required because the mind is not physically touched by sensible things and phantasms cannot by themselves determine an immaterial faculty. Yet human cognition is an immanent act — actio, not mere passio. Knowledge is not identical with the production or reception of a species; the living power must act.

An intentional species is a representational quality. It is not the external object itself, nor a material little picture transported into the mind. Suárez treats species as having their own accidental being, distinct from the actual act of perceiving or knowing. Species contribute effectively, but not formally, to cognition; they help determine the power toward an object, but the knowing itself is a vital act of the power.

In the case of intellect, the need for intelligible species is especially clear. Suárez argues that phantasms cannot sufficiently determine the intellect because they are material and belong to a lower order. The intellect must have its own spiritual species, distinct from sensible species. He rejects crude materialist accounts of phantasms as animal spirits or corpuscles in the brain and insists that intentional species are accidents or qualities rather than material particles.

This gives Suárez a layered theory of representation. External things affect the senses. Sense powers receive sensible species. Inner sense and imagination conserve and modify such sensory forms. The agent intellect produces intelligible species from or with reference to phantasms. The possible intellect then understands through spiritual species. At no point is cognition merely mechanical transmission. Each level requires the vital activity of the relevant power.

8. Cognitive acts as immanent, vital, and attention-dependent

Suárez distinguishes the cognitive act from both the faculty and the species. He discusses the view that cognition is a vital reception of species, but he argues that a cognitive act is a special quality really distinct from the faculty as informed by species. This lets him explain why a power may possess species without actually thinking of every object represented by them. The faculty does not always understand all the species it has because it does not always attend to or vitally use them.

This is philosophically significant. Suárez has resources for distinguishing dispositional representation from occurrent cognition. A species can be present as a representational disposition or quality, while an act of attention or use is required for actual cognition. In modern terms, he distinguishes something like stored representational content from active mental episode. But he does so without reducing the active episode to a functional activation pattern. It is a vital act of a power rooted in the soul.

The same structure applies to perception. The mere reception of a species is not yet apprehension. After reception there follows an act in which the thing is vitally conceived or known. Apprehension may be simple or comparative, and the intellect can combine concepts. Suárez therefore offers an account in which representation, attention, apprehension, and judgment are distinguishable but integrated.

9. External senses, internal senses, and animal cognition

Suárez follows the scholastic tradition in distinguishing external senses from internal senses. The internal senses include common sense, phantasy, imagination, estimative or cogitative power, memory, and reminiscence, though he discusses the standard scholastic question of how many such senses should be posited. Common sense knows the proper objects of the external senses and discerns among them; one external sense alone cannot discern, for example, color and sound.

Phantasy receives and conserves sensible species. Imagination adds the ability to compose sensible forms and to feign even impossible objects. The common scholastic position identifies four internal senses, but Suárez’s own treatment is functionally nuanced: he is interested less in multiplying faculties for its own sake than in explaining what operations must be accounted for.

The internal senses matter because Suárez refuses to make animals mere automata. Brutes have sensation, inner sensory awareness, memory-like retention, and practical estimative capacities. But he also refuses to attribute properly intellectual judgment and reasoning to them. He denies that exterior sense judges by composing and dividing. Common sense can discriminate differences simply, but brutes do not judge by intellectual composition and division; otherwise, they would reason.

This gives Suárez a graded theory of mind. Animals have genuine cognition, but not intellect. Human beings share sensory cognition with animals, but human intellectual cognition introduces universality, judgment, discursive inference, reflexive self-knowledge, and free will. Angels and God, in turn, possess intellect without the embodied sensory mediation that characterizes human cognition.

10. Sensory consciousness and inner awareness

A major contemporary debate concerns Suárez on consciousness and self-awareness. Dominik Perler’s interpretation, as summarized in the IEP and PhilPapers, stresses that Suárez distinguishes first-order sensory consciousness from second-order intellectual consciousness, and explains the unity of consciousness through a single soul with hierarchically ordered faculties. Daniel Heider’s work on Suárez’s imaginative power and perceptual self-awareness adds an important nuance: Suárez does not simply deny all sensory self-awareness, but he sharply distinguishes sensory awareness from intellectual reflection.

Suárez rejects the idea that a sense power can properly and distinctly cognize its own operation in the way intellect can. Internal sense can perceive only objects received through external sense, and self-awareness in the strict intellectual sense exceeds the radius of sensory powers. But this does not mean that sensory life is blind from the inside. Suárez allows a kind of concomitant awareness in actu exercito: the sentient being is aware in the exercise of sensing. This is not a separate reflective act; it is rooted in the vital soul’s presence in attentive operations.

This is one of the places where Suárez is especially interesting for contemporary philosophy of mind. He has neither a simple higher-order theory, according to which all consciousness requires a second-order act, nor a simple first-order representational theory, according to which conscious awareness is exhausted by object-representation. Instead, he distinguishes levels: object-directed sensory awareness, concomitant vital self-presence, inner-sense awareness of sensory operations through modified species, and intellectual reflection on acts, species, and the soul itself.

11. Agent intellect, possible intellect, and abstraction

Suárez’s account of intellectual cognition depends on the distinction between agent intellect and possible intellect, but he interprets that distinction in a distinctive way. The agent intellect is not itself the faculty that understands. It is called “intellect” because it produces intelligible species; as such, it does not understand. The possible intellect, by contrast, is the power whose act is vital and immanent understanding. Suárez rejects Greek and Averroist-style views that treat agent and possible intellect as separated substances rather than powers of the human soul.

Spanish scholarship on Suárez’s agent intellect emphasizes this point: the agent intellect is a non-cognizing faculty whose role is limited to the illumination of images and the production of intelligible species. Suárez also criticizes Cajetan’s account of an illuminative action on phantasms prior to the production of intelligible species, regarding that action as obscure or unintelligible. He reduces the supposed multiple operations of agent intellect to one operation described under several names: it makes intelligibles in act by producing intelligible species.

The metaphysics of abstraction follows from this. The phantasm does not efficiently produce the intelligible species. Rather, the intellective power, through agent intellect, produces a spiritual species corresponding to the object represented in the phantasm. The phantasm is necessary in the present human state, but it is not an efficient cause operating upward into the immaterial intellect. This is one of Suárez’s most important departures from simpler Aristotelian accounts of abstraction.

12. Universals, singulars, and intellectual knowledge

Suárez agrees that intellect is ordered to universals, but he does not conclude that intellect knows only universals. He argues that in a proposition such as “Peter is a man,” the intellect must know not only the universal predicate but also the singular subject and the copula. Therefore the intellect can know singulars as well as universals. Sense knows only singulars; intellect knows both singulars and universals.

This matters for any extraction of a Suárezian philosophy of mind. If intellect knew only abstract universals, human thought would be disconnected from concrete persons and actions. But Suárez’s intellect can return to singulars through a quasi-reflexive act. It can think not only “humanity” but “this human being,” not only “goodness” but “this good to be chosen,” not only “soul” but “my soul acting now.”

His account therefore combines abstraction with intentional return. The intellect abstracts universal natures from phantasms, but it can also know singulars by reflecting on the relation between universal content, sensory presentation, and the act of cognition. This is central to judgment, action, and self-knowledge.

13. Apprehension, judgment, and truth

Suárez’s account of judgment is subtle. He distinguishes simple apprehension from comparative cognition, but he also allows a broad sense of judgment already present in simple perceptual cognition. In seeing white, the same act may judge in actu exercito that what is seen is white.

He rejects the claim that judgment must always be a separate reflex act. Ordinary people judge without explicitly reflecting on their own judgments. Judgment is not necessarily a higher-order cognition of one’s cognition; it is the power’s adhesion to the known thing by an act elicited by that power. In the stricter intellectual sense, judgment is the act by which the intellect knows that a predicate belongs to a subject. But even here Suárez resists multiplying acts unnecessarily: judgment is not distinct from cognitive composition in a way that would generate an infinite regress.

This is important for intentionality and consciousness. Suárez’s mental act is not merely a neutral representation waiting for a separate faculty to endorse it. Cognition tends toward truth through the power’s vital adhesion to the object. Intellectual judgment makes explicit the predicative structure of truth, but even simple cognition has a primitive affirmative orientation toward its object.

14. Beings of reason and the mind’s object-making power

Suárez’s metaphysics of entia rationis, or beings of reason, belongs directly to his philosophy of mind. A being of reason is not a real being outside the mind, but it has objective being in the intellect. Suárez’s Disputationes Metaphysicae treats real beings and beings of reason separately, but he insists that a metaphysics of real being still has to explain beings of reason as “shadows of beings.”

The key point is that the mind can think what has no real entity in itself: blindness, negations, privations, impossibilities, chimeras, relations constructed by reason, and other objects that exist only as objects of thought. To deny that such things exist-in-thought while knowingly talking about them would be self-contradictory.

This adds an important dimension to Suárez’s philosophy of mind: intentionality is not limited to tracking real external things. The intellect can objectify, compare, negate, relate, and construct. It can think nonbeing under the form of being. It can also form logical and metaphysical objects that are indispensable for science, even though they are not real beings in the same way substances, qualities, or acts are.

15. Self-knowledge and reflexivity

Self-knowledge is one of Suárez’s strongest arguments for the spirituality of intellect. The intellect can know itself, its acts, its species, and its intellectual light. But Suárez carefully distinguishes human self-knowledge in the present embodied state from the self-knowledge of separated substances. Spiritual things are intelligible in themselves, but our intellect in its present condition does not match the mode of separated intelligences. Immaterial beings that are wholly without matter can be identical with what they know in a stronger way; our intellect, currently joined to the body, knows itself as it knows other intelligibles, through acts and species rather than by immediate angelic self-possession.

This makes Suárez’s account more modest than some Platonizing theories of intellectual self-transparency. The human intellect is reflexive, but not absolutely transparent to itself. It knows itself through its operations. Self-knowledge is real and philosophically decisive, but it is mediated.

The contemporary literature has rightly treated this as a major theme. An Oxford chapter on Suárez and self-awareness summarizes the issue as involving whether the sensitive soul is aware of its own acts, how the intellectual soul knows itself, and whether the soul after death knows itself in a disembodied state. This triad — sensory self-awareness, intellectual self-reflection, separated self-knowledge — is exactly the structure a comprehensive Suárezian philosophy of mind needs.

16. Will, intellect, and rational appetite

The will is not an afterthought in Suárez. It is one of the two principal rational powers, alongside intellect. Its object is the good in common, and it follows intellect insofar as the will requires cognition of a thing as good.

Suárez distinguishes many acts of will: love, desire, intention, fruition, consent, choice, use, and acts concerning ends and means. He also distinguishes elicited acts of the will from commanded acts, in which the will moves other powers. This gives him a theory of action in which cognition and appetite are internally related but not collapsed into one another.

The relation between intellect and will is complex. The intellect moves the will finaliter, by presenting an object as good; the will moves the intellect efficaciter, by directing attention or use. But this motion is moral rather than a direct physical causation. Suárez also says that the intellect directs and commands the will, while the will moves the intellect and disposes it in free acts.

This reciprocal relation gives Suárez a sophisticated model of deliberation. The intellect presents reasons and goods. The will inclines, chooses, commands, and can redirect intellectual attention. Human action is neither blind volition nor deterministic cognition. It is a coordinated act of rational appetite and practical understanding.

17. Freedom

Suárez’s account of freedom is a core part of his philosophy of mind and action. The will follows rational and discursive power and is therefore free. The will is not necessitated with respect to exercise in this life; the intellect, by contrast, is not free in the same way, because it can be necessitated by evident truth. The will is not similarly necessitated by evident good.

This contrast between intellect and will is philosophically rich. Truth compels the intellect under conditions of evidence. Goodness does not compel the will in exactly the same way, at least in the present life. The will can choose, resist, suspend, or redirect action, even when the intellect presents a good. Suárez therefore preserves a robust account of free agency.

He also integrates this with divine concurrence. Divine cooperation accommodates the nature of each cause and cooperates with liberty without necessitating the will. Suárez also holds that the will can resist astral and sensitive influences. This is not a merely theological claim; it is a theory of agency under layered causal conditions. Bodily, sensory, cosmic, and divine conditions may be present, but the will remains a free rational power.

18. Intellect versus will: hierarchy and dignity

Suárez discusses whether intellect or will is more perfect. He sides with the broadly Thomistic position that intellect is more perfect. The natural dignity of human beings comes primarily from intellect; the will is rational by participation from intellect, and intellect is more perfect because it can be elevated to a more perfect act.

This does not make the will secondary in practical life. Suárez’s own action theory gives the will a decisive role in free exercise, attention, choice, command, and resistance to inclination. But at the level of formal dignity, intellect has priority because it discloses being and truth, and because will depends on the intellectual presentation of the good.

A Suárezian philosophy of mind should therefore avoid two distortions. It should not intellectualize action so much that will becomes a mere conclusion of reasoning. But it also should not voluntarize the mind so much that intellect becomes an instrument of arbitrary choice. Suárez’s position is relational: intellect specifies and presents; will exercises, chooses, and commands.

19. The mind of God

The mind of God is indispensable for Suárez’s general theory of intellect. When intellect is considered absolutely, Suárez says the discussion may include separated substances and especially the first being. Divine intellect is therefore not an external theological topic added after the “real” philosophy is done. It is the maximal case of intellect: immaterial, non-discursive, self-identical, creative, and exemplar.

This matters especially for intentionality and representation. Suárez says that representational species are likenesses by essence and that representation is intentional rather than a matter of material lines or pictorial resemblance. He adds that the divine substance, because it is more spiritual, is in a sense omnirepresentative. The divine mind is thus not merely another mind with more information. It is the ultimate case of intellectual representation: God knows all things through himself, not through bodily species, phantasms, or discursive abstraction.

This has a direct philosophical payoff. In human cognition, species represent objects because they are intentional likenesses. In divine cognition, the divine essence itself is the supreme intelligible ground of representation. That gives Suárez a metaphysical theory of mental content: finite intentionality is not brute picturing, but a structured participation in intelligibility whose maximal case is divine self-knowledge and knowledge of creatures.

Divine mind also matters for possibility, essence, and beings of reason. If the intellect can objectify non-real or merely possible beings, then the question arises how such objects are grounded. Suárez’s divine intellect provides a metaphysical horizon for possible essences, exemplar causation, and the intelligibility of creatures. Contemporary work on Suárez’s concepts and divine-human intellection explicitly treats the intertwining of divine and human intellection as a major issue.

20. Angelic minds and separated intellects

Angels are equally important as comparative cases. They are created intellects without bodies. Suárez says that an angel constantly knows itself, whereas human beings chiefly come to self-knowledge through the science of the soul. This contrast helps Suárez define what is distinctive about human mind: humans have spiritual intellect, but they do not know in the manner of pure intelligences while joined to the body.

Human knowledge of God and separated substances in the present life is possible but limited. Suárez says our intellect can attain some knowledge of them, yet its proportionate object is the quiddity of material things, and without phantasms nothing is understood in the present state. He compares our relation to spiritual substances to the weak vision of owls before the sun.

That analogy is crucial. Suárez is not saying that God and angels are irrelevant because humans cannot know them directly in the present life. He is saying that they exceed our natural cognitive mode. We can know them indirectly, analogically, and imperfectly from sensible traces and intellectual reflection. This is a theory of cognitive limitation, not a dismissal of nonhuman minds.

Angels also clarify what human intellect is not. Human intellect is not a separated substance operating independently of the body, because in the present state it depends on phantasms. But it is also not a sensory faculty, because it can know universals, truth, being, nonbeing, itself, and God. Human mind is intermediate: embodied like animal cognition, spiritual like angelic cognition, but identical with neither.

21. The separated human soul

The separated soul is one of the most important test cases for Suárez’s philosophy of mind. In his disputation on the separated soul, he holds that the same numerical soul remains after separation, that it is still truly a soul, and that it remains ordered to vivify the body even though actual vivification is not essential to its continued existence. In the separated soul, only intellect and will remain as operative powers.

This shows that Suárez’s human soul is subsistent but incomplete. It can exist without the body, but it remains naturally ordered to the body. It is not converted into an angel. Its separated condition reveals the spiritual independence of intellect and will, but also the unnatural or at least non-standard character of disembodiment for a human soul.

Suárez discusses whether the separated soul naturally has or acquires species, and he resists explaining its post-mortem knowledge simply by supernatural infusion. He also asks how separated souls and angels might communicate, and whether they can know thoughts and free operations. These questions are not speculative excess if we are reconstructing his philosophy of mind. They concern non-embodied cognition, mental communication, privacy of thought, intellectual species without phantasms, and the continuity of personal identity after bodily separation.

For Suárez, the separated soul reveals both the power and the limitation of human mind. It confirms that intellect and will are not organ-bound. But it also confirms that the human soul is not naturally a complete separate substance in the way an angel is. Human mind is spiritual, but its natural mode is incarnate.

22. Toward a systematic Suárezian philosophy of mind

A comprehensive reconstruction can be organized around seven theses.

First, the ontological thesis: the human mind is grounded in one rational soul, the substantial form of the living human body. This soul is the principle of life, unity, cognition, and free action. It is not reducible to the body, but it is not a complete Cartesian substance simply added to the body.

Second, the powers thesis: mental life is structured by really distinct powers rooted in the soul. Intellect, will, external sense, internal sense, imagination, memory, and appetite are stable principles of operation. They are not independent agents, and they do not form a mere aggregate. The unity of the subject comes from the one soul.

Third, the act thesis: cognition is a vital, immanent act. Species are necessary for object-determination, but they are not cognition itself. The living power must attend, use, apprehend, judge, and act.

Fourth, the intentionality thesis: mental acts are object-directed through intentional species, concepts, phantasms, and intellectual operations. Representation is not material resemblance. It is an intentional relation grounded in spiritual or sensory forms. The intellect can also objectify beings of reason, negations, privations, and impossibilities.

Fifth, the consciousness thesis: consciousness is layered. There is sensory awareness, concomitant vital self-presence, inner-sense awareness, intellectual reflection, and higher self-knowledge in separated intellects. Human self-awareness is real but mediated by acts.

Sixth, the agency thesis: rational action is produced by the interaction of intellect and will. Intellect presents truth and good; will chooses, commands, and can move intellect in the order of exercise. Freedom is not mere indeterminacy; it is the self-determining power of rational appetite under intellectual presentation of the good.

Seventh, the comparative-mind thesis: human mind must be understood between animal cognition below and angelic and divine intellect above. Animals have genuine sensory cognition but lack intellective judgment and discursive reason. Angels have created intellect without bodily dependence. God is the supreme intellect whose self-knowledge and omnirepresentative essence form the maximal case of mind.

23. Suárez compared with later philosophy of mind

Suárez is not a Cartesian dualist, though he helps explain the background from which Cartesian philosophy emerged. Late scholastics often described operations of intellect as concepts, while Descartes and his followers increasingly used the language of ideas; recent work on Suárez and Descartes treats Suárez as part of this transition.

The difference from Cartesianism is substantial. Suárez does not identify mind with a thinking substance complete in itself. The human soul is the substantial form of the body. It is subsistent because of its spiritual operations, but it is naturally ordered to embodiment. Thinking is not the essence of a detached ego; it is the act of an intellectual power rooted in the rational soul.

Suárez is also not a materialist. Intellectual cognition abstracts from material conditions, knows universals and nonentities, reflects on itself, and directs free action. No bodily organ performs these acts formally. Yet Suárez is not hostile to embodiment: he repeatedly stresses that in the present state humans require senses and phantasms for intellectual knowledge. Bodily injury, sensory impairment, fatigue, and imaginative failure can impede understanding, not because intellect is a brain-function, but because the embodied human intellect normally works through sensory mediation.

In contemporary categories, Suárez is closest to a hylomorphic non-reductivism. Mental life is not an emergent property of matter alone, nor a separate immaterial substance accidentally paired with a machine-body. It is the activity of a living substantial form whose higher powers exceed matter while remaining naturally embodied.

24. Major interpretive tensions

The first tension concerns causal coordination. Suárez denies that sensory powers efficiently cause intellectual acts, but he also requires phantasms for human intellection in the present state. His solution is that lower powers occasion or provide quasi-exemplars for higher acts, while the soul coordinates the whole. This preserves the immateriality of intellect, but it leaves a difficult question: what exactly secures the determinate correspondence between phantasm and intelligible species?

The second tension concerns representational grounding. Suárez says species represent by their intentional nature, not by material resemblance. This is powerful, but it may seem to leave representation primitive. His theology of divine intellect helps deepen the account by making intelligibility metaphysically grounded in divine exemplar knowledge, but that answer depends on accepting the broader metaphysical-theological system.

The third tension concerns self-awareness. Suárez grants sensory concomitant awareness and inner-sense awareness, while reserving strict reflective self-knowledge for intellect. This layered model is subtle, but it raises boundary questions. How much awareness can be present without intellectual reflection? How does the vital soul’s presence in acts generate phenomenal awareness? Contemporary scholarship rightly treats this as one of the most fertile areas in Suárez studies.

The fourth tension concerns the separated soul. Suárez wants to say the separated soul is truly the same soul and can operate intellectually and voluntarily without the body. But he also wants to say the human soul is naturally ordered to body and is not an angel. The separated soul therefore occupies a metaphysically awkward but philosophically illuminating middle position: subsistent but incomplete, spiritual but naturally embodied, capable of non-bodily intellection yet still human.

25. Research agenda for extracting Suárez’s philosophy of mind

A serious reconstruction should proceed in five stages.

The first stage is textual: establish the relevant corpus across the De anima, the Disputationes Metaphysicae, works on God, angels, will, and human action. The De anima gives the psychological architecture; the metaphysical disputations give the ontology of being, beings of reason, causality, and accidents; the theological works expand divine, angelic, and separated cognition.

The second stage is conceptual: define Suárez’s technical terms without translating them too quickly into modern categories. Species is not a biological species or a picture. Potentia is not merely a functional capacity. Actus is not merely behavior. Anima is not merely consciousness. Mens is not simply “the mind” in a Cartesian sense.

The third stage is comparative: map Suárez’s position against Aquinas, Scotus, Cajetan, Ockhamist and nominalist currents, Peter John Olivi, and the Conimbricenses. A Conimbricenses entry notes that Suárez’s psychology borrows from nominalist traditions and from opponents of Thomist psychology such as Olivi, while still working within a broadly Aristotelian-scholastic framework.

The fourth stage is multilingual scholarship. Spanish work such as Andrés Grau i Arau’s study of the agent intellect is especially useful for Suárez’s epistemology and abstraction. English-language work by Perler, Leijenhorst, Shields, and others is essential for consciousness, self-awareness, and early modern reception. German and Central European scholarship, reflected in bibliographies listing Rode on inner experience and Heider on perceptual self-awareness, is important for the phenomenological and consciousness-related dimensions.

The fifth stage is systematic: reconstruct Suárez not merely as a commentator on Aristotle, but as offering a theory of mental acts, representation, self-awareness, freedom, embodiment, and comparative intellect. That reconstruction must keep God, angels, and separated souls in view, because Suárez’s own theory of intellect requires them as metaphysical and cognitive limit cases.

26. Bottom-line reconstruction

Suárez’s philosophy of mind can be stated as follows.

The human mind is the rational soul operating through intellect and will within a living body. The soul is the substantial form and unity-principle of the human being, and its powers are real, stable, ordered principles of operation. Sensation is genuinely cognitive but organ-dependent. Imagination and inner sense conserve, combine, and mediate sensible forms. Intellect depends on phantasms in the present state but produces and uses spiritual intelligible species; its acts are vital, immanent, reflexive, truth-oriented, and capable of knowing universals, singulars, beings of reason, itself, God, and separated substances. Will is rational appetite directed toward the good in common; it is free in exercise, able to command other powers, and not necessitated by sensory inclination or even by evident finite good.

God and angels complete the picture. God is the supreme intellect, whose essence is maximally intelligible and omnirepresentative. Angels are created separated intellects, capable of self-knowledge without bodily mediation. The separated human soul shows that intellect and will can survive bodily separation, but also that human mind remains naturally ordered to embodiment. Suárez therefore gives neither a flat naturalistic psychology nor an escapist theology of mind. He gives a hierarchical metaphysics of cognition: plant life, animal awareness, human rationality, angelic intellect, and divine mind form an ordered field in which human consciousness is intelligible precisely because it is both embodied and spiritual.

PreviousPietro Pomponazzi NextThomas Hobbes

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
